WASHINGTON, D. c., May 49,


What effect does prohibition have


on city of 100000 inhabitants, in


which it has been the custom to
spend $2,5OO,OO0 annually for
liquor?


The saloon men and the prohibi-
tionists have been carrying on a
red hot controversy over this ques-
ton before the house committee on
the District of Columbia ever since
the sixtieth congress has been in
session.


The prohibitionists have proven
decisively that prohibition de-
creases crime and increases saw
ings accounts.


Kansas City, Kas., has been on
trial. 1t is the largest prohibition


city in the United States. The
liquor men made Kansas City take
the center of the stage by introduce
ing as evidence to show why Wash
ington should not be made dry" a
newspaper article representing
Kansas City to be hopelessly ban


krupt. 1t declares houses once fill
ed are empty, that murders have
become numerous with prohibition,
that thieves operate at will and
that it is necessary for the pack
ing firms to pay the salaries of pO-
licemen and firemen. The picture
of desolation seemed on the face


of it a mighty argument for sa-
looms.


Matthew O'Brien, representing
the national prohibition party,
showed that the article had been
written at the order of the brewers,


and had been supplied fee to news


papers all over the country through


tainted news bureau.
O'Brien showed that the assess
ed valuation of the city in 1906 was
1881, 1850, and in 1907 $685.
$40, showing gain in real estate
values instead of decrease because
of closed saloons. The total city tax
for all purposes for 1906 was $40
on each 800 of assessed valuation,


while for 1907 the city tax rate was
$500, a reduction OF 20 cents with
all Joints" closed.


The population of Kansas City,


Kas., at present, is estimated at
100, 1886, an increase over 1906 of
11,1S0.


In direct contrast to a showing
made in tainted" article printed
in the Washington Post, March 2,
